model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"delete_exact","function":"","old":"","new":"","anchor":"","text":"import math","source":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"The warmup-cosine schedule significantly regressed loss under this 256-step budget, so the safest high-value next move is to revert to no scheduler and recover the stronger baseline optimization behavior.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Under a strict 256-step budget, Adam often benefits from a larger initial step size; doubling the learning rate is a simple, high-signal change that can improve early loss descent without adding complexity.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The previous LR increase improved validation loss, suggesting the run remains optimization-limited at 256 steps; a moderate follow-up increase tests whether faster early progress yields additional gains without a large stability jump.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Loss kept improving as LR increased, indicating additional headroom in this short-budget regime; moving to 2e-3 is a controlled next step to accelerate early optimization further.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"2e-3 overshot and degraded validation loss, so reverting to 1.5e-3 restores the current best-performing learning-rate region under the fixed short budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"With only 256 optimization steps, explicit L2 regularization can slow early fitting more than it helps generalization; removing weight decay is a simple test to improve short-horizon validation loss.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Removing weight decay worsened loss, so restoring 1e-4 returns to the stronger regularization setting near the current best configuration.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Optimization tuning appears near a local optimum, so a modest capacity increase is the next low-complexity lever; raising base channels to 16 can improve representational power without a drastic compute jump under the same step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Increasing model width hurt validation loss under the fixed short budget, so reverting to the smaller baseline capacity restores the better-performing compute/optimization balance.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"After tuning learning rate, switching from Adam to AdamW is a focused optimizer-family test that can improve generalization with the same short-step budget while keeping the rest of the configuration unchanged.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"AdamW produced slightly worse validation loss than Adam at the tuned learning rate, so reverting preserves the stronger optimizer choice before exploring other levers.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With a fixed number of optimizer steps, increasing batch size can improve gradient quality and expose more unique samples within the same step budget, which may reduce validation loss in this short training regime.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After the large gain from increasing batch size, the optimizer may tolerate a higher step size; testing 2e-3 is the most direct follow-up to exploit the new batch regime for further short-horizon loss reduction.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"At batch size 128, increasing LR to 2e-3 degraded validation loss versus the prior best, so reverting to 1.5e-3 restores the strongest known point in this regime.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The jump from batch size 64 to 128 produced the largest gain so far, suggesting this fixed-step setting benefits from processing more examples per step; testing 256 is the highest-impact next scaling probe.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The much larger batch size likely reduces gradient noise, so the setup may support a higher learning rate than before; increasing to 2e-3 is a targeted follow-up to improve convergence speed within the same 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Performance has improved consistently with larger batch sizes in this fixed-step evaluator, so a further increase to 384 is a high-upside continuation while staying less extreme than a jump to 512.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After strong gains from scaling batch size to 384, a modest LR increase is the safest remaining high-upside test to better match the lower-noise gradient regime without further increasing runtime.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Increasing LR to 2.5e-3 worsened validation loss, so reverting to 2e-3 restores the best-performing learning-rate setting found with batch size 384.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}
